coredata.dc:title: Effects of Moringa oleifera leaf-derived carbon dots treatment on ROS metabolism regulation for postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage preservation

coredata.dc:description: Postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage is prone to rapid senescence and quality deterioration during storage at room temperature. The imbalance of reactive oxygen species (ROS) metabolism is one of the critical factors leading to this process. In this study, we synthesized Moringa oleifera leaf-derived carbon dots (MoCDs) and investigated the effects of MoCDs treatment on ROS metabolism regulation for postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage preservation. The results showed that MoCDs exhibited more efficient antioxidant activity than M. oleifera extract, attributed to their abundant surface functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups, and an extensive sp2-hybridized carbon network. Moreover, MoCDs treatment could reduce the accumulation of hydrogen peroxide and superoxide anion radical in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage by generally enhancing the activities of a series of key antioxidant enzymes and increasing the contents of non-enzymatic antioxidants, which significantly delayed leaf senescence and deterioration of appearance and nutritional quality of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage during storage. In summary, as an effective antioxidant, MoCDs entered leaf cells to scavenge excessive ROS directly; as an elicitor, MoCDs enhanced the ROS scavenging ability of the endogenous antioxidant defense system in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage. These dual protective mechanisms synergistically regulated ROS metabolism, thus benefiting the postharvest preservation of flowering Chinese cabbage.

(a–e) Contents of non-enzymatic antioxidants including total phenolic compounds (a), total flavonoids (b), and tocopherols (c). (d–f) •OH scavenging rate (d), DPPH• scavenging rate (e), and FRAP value (f) of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage. Control: flowering Chinese cabbage treated with ultrapure water. MoCDs: flowering Chinese cabbage treated with MoCDs (0.1 mg/mL). Each value is the mean of three biological replicates, and vertical bars represent the standard error (SE) of means. Asterisks indicate significant differences between the control and MoCDs treatment at each storage time point according to the Student's t-test (P < 0.05).

Schematic of the synthesis of MoCDs and the underlying mechanism of MoCDs for postharvest preservation of flowering Chinese cabbage. (a) Illustration of the synthesis of MoCDs. MoCDs: Moringa oleifera leaf-derived carbon dots. (b) The underlying mechanism of MoCDs for delaying senescence and quality deterioration of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage by regulating ROS metabolism. Postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage is uniformly sprayed with 0.1 mg/mL of MoCDs, and then MoCDs enter leaf cells. In leaf cells, MoCDs regulate ROS metabolism in two ways: (i) MoCDs directly scavenge excessive ROS; (ii) MoCDs enhance the endogenous antioxidant defense system of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage to scavenge excessive ROS. These two ways work together to enhance the antioxidant ability of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage treated with MoCDs, thereby reducing ROS accumulation and delaying senescence and quality deterioration of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage.

head.ce:title: Effects of Moringa oleifera leaf-derived carbon dots treatment on ROS metabolism regulation for postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage preservation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage is prone to rapid senescence and quality deterioration during storage at room temperature. The imbalance of reactive oxygen species (ROS) metabolism is one of the critical factors leading to this process. In this study, we synthesized Moringa oleifera leaf-derived carbon dots (MoCDs) and investigated the effects of MoCDs treatment on ROS metabolism regulation for postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage preservation. The results showed that MoCDs exhibited more efficient antioxidant activity than M. oleifera extract, attributed to their abundant surface functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups, and an extensive sp2-hybridized carbon network. Moreover, MoCDs treatment could reduce the accumulation of hydrogen peroxide and superoxide anion radical in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage by generally enhancing the activities of a series of key antioxidant enzymes and increasing the contents of non-enzymatic antioxidants, which significantly delayed leaf senescence and deterioration of appearance and nutritional quality of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage during storage. In summary, as an effective antioxidant, MoCDs entered leaf cells to scavenge excessive ROS directly; as an elicitor, MoCDs enhanced the ROS scavenging ability of the endogenous antioxidant defense system in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage. These dual protective mechanisms synergistically regulated ROS metabolism, thus benefiting the postharvest preservation of flowering Chinese cabbage.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The growing global population has increased the demand for fresh fruits and vegetables (Jiang et al., 2021). Among these, flowering Chinese cabbage (Brassica campestris L. ssp. chinensis var. utilis Tsen et Lee), originated in China and popular in Asia, is rich in glucosinolates and antioxidants like ascorbic acid, flavonoids, carotenoids, and phenolic compounds (Tan et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2022). It is often stored for a few days during transportation, marketing display, or after purchase, while its metabolism remains active (Meitha et al., 2020). This leads to leaf yellowing, water loss, wilting, lignin content increase, rot and so on, resulting in nutrient loss, reduced market value, and a shortened shelf life, causing economic losses and resource waste (Kuang et al., 2023). Therefore, addressing these issues is necessary to meet the supply and demand in both domestic and export markets.

Reactive oxygen species (ROS) metabolism is vital in postharvest fruits and vegetables, and the imbalance of ROS homeostasis causes postharvest senescence and deterioration (Lin et al., 2021). The excessive accumulation of ROS leads to oxidative damage of membrane lipids, DNA, and proteins, thus adversely affecting the storage quality and shelf-life of postharvest products like longans (Liu et al., 2021), broccoli (Xu et al., 2021), and flowering Chinese cabbage (Lu et al., 2023). To mitigate oxidative stress caused by excessive ROS, plants activate their antioxidant defense system, comprising antioxidant enzymes and non-enzymatic antioxidants (Meitha et al., 2020). These two work synergically to protect plants from oxidative damage by scavenging ROS (Gill and Tuteja, 2010). Taking appropriate measures to reduce ROS accumulation in fruits and vegetables during storage and maintain ROS homeostasis is conducive to delaying senescence and preserving postharvest quality (Ali et al., 2024).

Currently, postharvest preservation strategies for fruits and vegetables mainly consist of physical, chemical, and biological techniques like cold storage, modified atmosphere packing, ultraviolet radiation, edible coatings, natural plant extracts, biological control agents and so on (Jiang et al., 2021; Palumbo et al., 2022). In recent years, nanotechnology has emerged as a novel strategy for the postharvest preservation of fruits and vegetables, and it offers multiple benefits such as protecting bioactive compounds, providing antioxidant properties, improving antimicrobial properties, developing intelligent packaging, and boosting the physical and mechanical properties of coatings (Fadiji et al., 2022; Iñiguez-Moreno et al., 2023). Among the numerous nanomaterials, biomass-derived carbon dots (CDs), as small-size (<10 nm), fluorescently stable, environmentally friendly, non-toxic, excellently biocompatible, sustainable, and tailorable carbon-based nanomaterials, have recently attracted a mounting interest (Xu et al., 2023). Biomass-derived CDs have found extensive applications in biological imaging, detection, cancer treatment, catalysis processes, energy transformation, and serving as carriers for medications (Fang et al., 2024; Wu et al., 2023). Moreover, in the field of fruit and vegetable preservation, some researchers have explored the application potential of CDs derived from biomass for postharvest preservation of fruits and vegetables (Guo et al., 2024; Kuang et al., 2023; Lu et al., 2023). However, there have not been systematic and in-depth studies on how biomass-derived CDs improve the preservation effect of fruits and vegetables, especially their potential association with ROS metabolism.

Using biomass materials enriched with antioxidant components as carbon sources is more likely to synthesize biomass-derived CDs with outstanding antioxidant ability and biocompatibility (Rodríguez-Varillas et al., 2022). Moringa oleifera Lam. (M. oleifera) leaves are a great source of antioxidants due to their abundant contents of polyphenols, ascorbic acid, α-Tocopherol, and beta-carotene (Kashyap et al., 2022). Therefore, we successfully synthesized M. oleifera leaf-derived carbon dots (MoCDs) and found they exhibited superior antioxidant activity compared to the M. oleifera extract. The objective of the present study was to investigate the effects of MoCDs treatment on the preservation of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage and systematically elucidate the underlying mechanism of MoCDs treatment on maintaining postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage quality from the perspective of ROS metabolism. Our results revealed that MoCDs could work synergically with endogenous antioxidant defense systems to regulate the ROS metabolism, thus effectively delaying the process of postharvest senescence and quality deterioration of flowering Chinese cabbage. Our findings could present a theoretical foundation for developing and applying novel biomass-derived CDs to prolong the shelf-life of fruits and vegetables.

Moringa oleifera leaf-derived carbon dots (MoCDs) were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method. In brief, fresh Moringa oleifera leaves were dried to constant weight in an oven at 40 °C and then crushed into powder. Moringa oleifera leaf powder (15 g) and ultrapure water (300 mL) were added to a beaker, followed by treatment with ultrasonication for 30 min to form a uniform suspension. Then, the suspension was heated at 180 °C for 6 h in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. When cooled to room temperature, large particles in the solution were removed by a 0.22 μm filter membrane. Further, the MoCDs were purified by dialyzing in a 1000 Da cellulose dialysis bag for 48 h, freeze-dried, and then stored at 4 °C until needed. And the characterization methods of MoCDs can be found in the Supplementary data.

The cytotoxicity of MoCDs was assessed via their effects on the cell viability of HeLa cells using thiazoyl blue tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assays. Detailed experimental materials and methods for the cell viability assay can be found in the Supplementary data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant properties of MoCDs and M. oleifera extract in vitro
ce:section.ce:para:
The scavenging activities of 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazylradical radical (DPPH•), hydroxyl radical (•OH), and superoxide anion radical (
O 2
• ‐
), as well as potassium permanganate (KMnO4) reduction experiments were used to evaluate the antioxidant properties of MoCDs and M. oleifera leaf water extract according to Li et al. (2021a) and Wang et al. (2020). Detailed experimental materials and methods were referred to the Supplementary data.

Fresh flowering Chinese cabbage (Brassica campestris L. ssp. chinensis var. utilis Tsen et Lee) was picked from the experimental base of South China Agricultural University in Guangzhou, Guangdong Province, China on February 28, 2023. Flowering Chinese cabbage with uniform color and size, free from flowering, pests, and diseases, was selected as experimental material. In a preliminary experiment, on days 2 and 8 of storage, 0.1 mg/mL MoCDs more effectively inhibited chlorophyll degradation and maintained a higher antioxidant activity in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage than other concentrations (0.05 mg/mL, 0.2 mg/mL, and 0.4 mg/mL), thus effectively delaying postharvest senescence (Fig. S1). Therefore, 0.1 mg/mL MoCDs treatment was used as a basis for further studies. The selected flowering Chinese cabbage was randomly assigned to two groups for control and MoCDs treatments, with three repetitions per group and 60 plants per repetition. The MoCDs treatment was uniformly sprayed with 0.1 mg/mL of MoCDs, while the control group was uniformly sprayed with ultrapure water. The spray dosage was 0.1 L per repetition. All of the treated samples were air-dried at 20 °C ± 1 °C for 2 h and subsequently placed into polyethylene anti-fog bags (size: 25 cm × 25 cm; thickness: 0.03 mm; with holes) with six plants per bag. Afterward, all flowering Chinese cabbage samples were stored at 20 °C ± 1 °C and 80–90% relative humidity (RH) for five days. From day 0, appearance attributes, weight loss, respiratory intensity, ethylene release rate, and histochemical detection in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage were assessed daily. Meanwhile, three bags of treated samples were taken daily from the control and the MoCDs treatments and were stored at −80 °C for the following measurements.

The total chlorophyll, chlorophyll a, and chlorophyll b contents were determined following the previous study and denoted as mg/g fresh weight (FW) (Lu et al., 2023). The weight loss rate of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage was calculated using the following formula: Weight loss rate (%) = [(initial weight - daily weight)/initial weight] × 100% (Xu et al., 2021). Meanwhile, the soluble protein content was measured using the Coomassie Brilliant Blue (G-250) method and was denoted as mg/g FW (Du et al., 2024). The soluble sugar content was assayed by anthrone colorimetry and was expressed as mg/g FW (Yu et al., 2022). The respiration intensity and ethylene release rate were measured by the canning method combined with gas chromatography. Detailed experimental materials and methods were referred to the Supplementary data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of ROS metabolism in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage
ce:section.ce:para:
Histochemical determination of H2O2 was detected by 3,3′-diaminobenzidine tetrahydrochloride (DAB) staining (Tan et al., 2020). Histochemical determination of
O 2
• ‐
was detected by nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NBT) staining (Tan et al., 2020). Detailed experimental materials and methods reffered to the Supplementary data.

The MDA content was measured by the MDA content kit (Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd, China) and expressed as nmol/g FW. Also, the H2O2 content was determined by the H2O2 content kit (Suzhou Grace Biotechnology Co., Ltd, China) and denoted as μmol/g FW. In addition, the
O 2
• ‐
production rate was determined by the hydroxylamine oxidation method (Xu et al., 2021). The
O 2
• ‐
production rate was expressed as nmol/(min • g FW). Detailed experimental materials and methods were referred to the Supplementary data.

We determined the following antioxidant enzyme activities. The superoxide dismutase (SOD) activity was measured using NBT method (Li et al., 2023). The peroxidase (POD) activity was determined using the Guaiacol method (Yu et al., 2022). The glutathione peroxidase (GPX) activity was measured by the previous method (Li et al., 2019). The CAT activity was determined using the catalase (CAT) activity assay kit (Suzhou Grace Biotechnology Co., Ltd., China). The activities of ascorbate peroxidase (APX), glutathione reductase (GR), dehydroascorbate reductase (DHAR) and monodehydroascorbate reductase (MDHAR) were measured following previous methods (Li et al., 2023; Yu et al., 2022). Detailed experimental materials and methods were referred to the Supplementary data.

We measured the following non-enzymatic antioxidant contents. The contents of ascorbate (AsA) and dehydroascorbate (DHA) were determined according to Li et al. (2023). The contents of reduced glutathione (GSH) and oxidized glutathione (GSSG) were measured using GSH and GSSG content detection kits (Hefei Laier Biotechnology Co., Ltd., China). Total phenolic, total flavonoid, and tocopherol contents were detected following previous research (Xu et al., 2021). Detailed experimental materials and methods were referred to the Supplementary data.

We assessed the antioxidant ability of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage by the •OH scavenging rate (Li et al., 2021a), DPPH• scavenging rate (Li et al., 2021a), and Ferric Reducing Antioxidant Power (FRAP) (Li et al., 2023). Detailed experimental materials and methods were referred to the Supplementary data.

All experiments followed a completely randomized design with three biological replicates. The results were expressed as mean ± standard errors (SE) (n = 3). The statistical analysis and presentation of the experimental data were conducted using OriginPro software, version 2021 (OriginLab, Northampton, Massachusetts, USA). Significant differences between different groups were analyzed using a One-way analysis of variance (one-way ANOVA) and Student's t-test at a significance level of P < 0.05. The asterisk ∗ in the figures indicated a significant difference (P < 0.05) between the MoCDs treatment and the control group at the same storage days.

MoCDs were synthesized through a hydrothermal treatment of M. oleifera leaf powder at 180 °C for 6 h. Structural properties of nanoparticles, like particle size and surface zeta potential, affected their transport through leaf stomata (with micron pore diameter), plant cell walls (with porosity ranging from 5 to 20 nm in diameter), and cell lipid bilayers (Lew et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2017). The morphologies of the MoCDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). As shown in Fig. 1 a and b, MoCDs were uniform and monodispersed spherical nanoparticles with mean diameters of 5.06 nm ± 0.11 nm. The inset HRTEM image of MoCDs displayed well-resolved lattice fringes with an interlayer spacing of 0.21 nm, which was ascribed to the (100) crystal plane of graphite, indicating the formation of graphene-like structures (Fig. 1a) (Li et al., 2022). Furthermore, the zeta potential of MoCDs in aqueous solution was −10.67 mV ± 1.33 mV (Fig. S2), which ensured high colloidal stability due to electrostatic repulsion between MoCDs (da Silva et al., 2021). The negative surface charge and colloidal stability of MoCDs were controlled mainly by hydrophilic groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amide groups (da Silva et al., 2021). Therefore, the negatively charged MoCDs were bound to the outer side of the positively charged leaf mesophyll cell membrane, and then the MoCDs were internalized into cells (Wu et al., 2017). These results suggested that MoCDs with small size and negative charge could infiltrate the stomata in the leaf surface and transport into leaf mesophyll cells.

Subsequently, the surface chemical structure of MoCDs was further characterized by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The FTIR spectrum of MoCDs (Fig. 1c) showed a broad absorption band at 3600-3100 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of O─H and N─H (Kou et al., 2021b). The peak at 2933 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibrations of C─H, and the characteristic peak around 1653 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibrations of C═O, C═N, and C═C (Kou et al., 2021a). The peak at 1408 cm−1 was assigned to C–N stretching vibrations, while the peaks in 1300 to 1000 cm−1 range were identified as the stretching vibration of C─O (Li et al., 2021b). The XPS survey spectra of MoCDs had three peaks located at 285.53 eV, 399.99 eV, and 532.28 eV, corresponding to C 1s (67.49%), N 1s (7.92%), and O 1s (24.59%), respectively (Fig. 1d). As shown in Fig. 1e–g, the high-resolution C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s XPS spectra respectively confirmed the presence of distinct bonds: C═C/C─C (284.80 eV), C─ O/C─N (286.26 eV), and C═O/C═N (288.15 eV) in the C 1s XPS spectra; pyridinic N (398.39 eV), pyrrolic N (399.84 eV), and graphitic N (401.61 eV) in the N 1s XPS spectra; and C═O (531.42 eV) and C─O (532.67 eV) in the O 1s XPS spectra (Wang et al., 2024). The above FTIR and XPS spectra demonstrated that the surface of MoCDs was rich in hydrophilic groups (─OH, ─COOH,─NH2, and amide), which accounted for the excellent aqueous dispersibility. Moreover, the surface functional groups of carbon dots correlate with their fluorescence and antioxidant activity (da Silva et al., 2021; Kou et al., 2021a).

The fluorescence of carbon dots is derived from conjugated sp2-carbon domains and affected by surface functional groups, causing redshift or blueshift of emission (Miao et al., 2018; Guo et al., 2022). We investigated the optical properties of the MoCDs via UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV–Vis absorption spectra displayed two absorption peaks at approximately 280 nm and 325 nm that could be attributed to π→π∗ transitions of sp2-hybridized carbon in the graphite carbon core and n→π∗ transitions of the surface chemical groups, respectively, indicating the formation of a conjugated graphite core with surface functional groups in MoCDs (Fig. 1h) (Li et al., 2021a). The inset pictures in Fig. 1h exhibited that the MoCDs aqueous solution was yellow under visible light and emitted a brightly blue fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet light. Under excitation wavelength from 325 nm to 405 nm, the emission spectra of MoCDs displayed excitation-wavelength-dependent emission properties (Fig. 1i). With the increase of excitation wavelength, the emission peaks gradually redshifted, indicating the existence of multiple fluorescence emission sites (Fig. 1i) (Chen et al., 2022; Li et al., 2021a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant activities of MoCDs and M. oleifera extract
ce:section.ce:para: The preservation of fruits and vegetables after harvesting is closely related to oxidative damage, and via the use of antioxidants, the senescence of vegetables can be delayed (Tan et al., 2020). It was reported that CDs with enriched function groups like ─OH, ─COOH,─NH2, and sp2-hybridized hybridized carbon domains exhibited excellent antioxidant activity and could reduce oxidative damage to plant cells (Kou et al., 2021a; Lu et al., 2023). And, many CDs inherit and amplify the inherent properties of synthetic precursors (Rodríguez-Varillas et al., 2022). According to this, we synthesized carbon dots from M. oleifera leaves to obtain nanoparticles with the antioxidant ability of the natural antioxidants in those leaves, and compared the antioxidant activities of MoCDs and M. oleifera leaf water extract.

KMnO4 was a potent oxidizer, and its reduction degree could evaluate the reducing ability of MoCDs, that is, the electron-donating ability of MoCDs (Wang et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. 2 a, MoCDs have a stronger reducing ability toward KMnO4 than the M. oleifera extract, and its half-maximal effective concentration (EC50) value is about 0.06 mg/mL, which is significantly lower than the EC50 value of the M. oleifera extract. This revealed that the electron-donating ability of MoCDs was superior compared to the M. oleifera extract, which was closely associated with the rich conjugated sp2-hybridized carbon domains of MoCDs (Ruiz et al., 2017). Next, DPPH•, a stable nitrogen-centered radical with a lone pair of electrons, was chosen to evaluate the total antioxidant activity of MoCDs. In the presence of antioxidants, the reduction process of DPPH• is primarily controlled by the hydrogen atom transfer mechanism, resulting in the formation of DPPH-H and the fading of the characteristic purple color of the solution (Ruiz et al., 2017). Fig. 2b suggested that the DPPH• scavenging rate of MoCDs was dose-dependent and superior to that of M. oleifera extract. Remarkably, the EC50 value of MoCDs toward DPPH• was about 0.06 mg/mL, outdoing many previously reported biomass-derived CDs (Deng et al., 2023; Huang et al., 2023; Li et al., 2021a). This result demonstrated that MoCDs had excellent hydrogen-donating ability, which was attributed to their abundant surface functional groups (e.g., the hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups) and extensive sp2-hybridized carbon network (Ji et al., 2019; Kou et al., 2021a; Ruiz et al., 2017).

In addition, considering that
O 2
• ‐
and •OH were the primary ROS inducing oxidative stress within plants (Kuang et al., 2023), we assessed the antioxidant activity of MoCDs against
O 2
• ‐
and •OH. It is reported that CDs exhibit superoxide dismutase (SOD)-like activity, and its catalytic activity depends on the binding of surface functional groups (e.g., ─OH, ─COOH,─NH2) with
O 2
• ‐
, as well as the electron transfer between
O 2
• ‐
and the carbonyl groups (C═O) conjugated with the π-system (Chen et al., 2025). To evaluate the
O 2
• ‐
scavenging activities of MoCDs and M. oleifera extract, the
O 2
• ‐
was produced by a riboflavin/NBT system (Chen et al., 2025). As shown in Fig. 2c, MoCDs exhibited significantly higher
O 2
• ‐
scavenging activity than M. oleifera extract, and both displayed dose-dependent responses. Notably, the EC50 value of MoCDs was about 0.7 mg/mL, which was 53% lower than that of M. oleifera extract, highlighting the excellent SOD-like activity of MoCDs. Furthermore, the •OH was generated by the Fenton reaction using ferrous sulfate and hydrogen peroxide as reactants (Kou et al., 2021a). The reduction of •OH involves electron transfer and hydrogen atom transfer process (Gong et al., 2023). We observed that the •OH scavenging activities of MoCDs and M. oleifera extract were also dose-dependent (Fig. 2d). The EC50 value of MoCDs was about 1.0 mg/mL, and decreased by approximately 20% compared to M. oleifera extract, surpassing previously reported biomass-derived CDs (Li et al., 2021a).

Overall, MoCDs exhibited more efficient antioxidant activity than M. oleifera extract, which could be attributed to the rich surface functional groups and large conjugated sp2-hybridized carbon framework of MoCDs. MoCDs were primarily involved in three antioxidant pathways: electron transfer, hydrogen atom transfer, and antioxidant enzyme-like catalysis (Gong et al., 2023). These three antioxidant pathways functioned individually or synergistically to scavenge ROS. Based on the above results, we speculated that MoCDs possessed the potential to delay postharvest senescence of fruits and vegetables by reducing oxidative damage.

Before applying MoCDs to fruit and vegetable preservation, it is essential to determine their biocompatibility. The cytotoxicity of MoCDs was assessed using the MTT assay on HeLa cells, revealing that the cell viability exceeded 85% at a concentration of 0.4 mg/mL (Fig. S3). This indicated that MoCDs were safe for plants and exhibited good biocompatibility at concentrations of 0.4 mg/mL and below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MoCDs treatment delay postharvest senescence and quality deterioration of flowering Chinese cabbage
ce:section.ce:para: It is reported that oxidative degradation often serves as one of the primary culprits in postharvest product quality deterioration (Guo et al., 2024). We verified the excellent in vitro antioxidant activities and low cytotoxicity of MoCDs. Nevertheless, it is still unidentified whether MoCDs can effectively delay postharvest senescence and quality deterioration of fruits and vegetables in practical applications. To study the effects of MoCDs on postharvest preservation of flowering Chinese cabbage (Brassica campestris L. ssp. chinensis var. utilis Tsen et Lee), we monitored a series of physiological and biochemical indexes related to appearance and nutritional quality.

Flowering Chinese cabbage is a raw leafy vegetable, and leaf senescence is primarily marked by yellowing due to chlorophyll degradation (Tan et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. 3 a, MoCDs treatment delayed the onset of yellowing from day 2 to day 3 compared to the control group. Chlorophyll content declined in both treatments over time; however, MoCDs treatment significantly inhibited chlorophyll degradation throughout storage (Fig. 3b). After five days of storage, the total chlorophyll content in the MoCDs treatment was 1.27 times that of the control. Similar patterns were observed for chlorophyll a and b (Fig. 3c; Fig. S4). These results indicated that MoCDs treatment positively delayed leaf senescence in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage by inhibiting chlorophyll degradation.

The plant hormone ethylene is a positive regulator of postharvest senescence in fruits and vegetables, and its accumulation promotes respiration, thus accelerating nutrient consumption and reducing storage tolerance (Ghimire et al., 2024). The ethylene release rate of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage increased and then decreased during senescence (Fig. 3d). The control peaked on day 2, while the MoCDs treatment peaked on day 3, with the MoCDs showing a significantly lower peak value than the control. Simultaneously, respiration intensity peaked on day 4 for both treatments but remained consistently lower in the MoCDs treatment, with a 17.21% reduction in the peak compared to the control (Fig. 3e). Therefore, MoCDs treatment could delay the ethylene peak, and reduce respiratory intensity during storage, thus delaying the senescence of harvested flowering Chinese cabbage.

To evaluate the nutritional quality of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage, we measured weight loss, soluble sugar, and soluble protein content. After harvest, fruits and vegetables lose nutrients and water due to active respiration and transpiration, resulting in weight loss, wilting, and reduced storage durability (Bajaj et al., 2023). As shown in Fig. 3f, the weight loss rate increased over time in both control and MoCDs treatments, but MoCDs treatment significantly reduced this rate of flowering Chinese cabbage, effectively alleviating postharvest wilting (Fig. 3a). Soluble sugars are fundamental substrates for respiration and important quality indicators for horticultural crops (Kuang et al., 2023). The soluble sugar content in both treatments increased sharply, peaked on day 3, and rapidly decreased (Fig. 3g). From days 3–5, the MoCDs treatment maintained significantly higher soluble sugar levels than the control. These results indicated that MoCDs promoted the hydrolysis of organic macromolecules into soluble small molecules in the early storage and reduced soluble sugar consumption by inhibiting respiration intensity during the later storage period. In addition, MoCDs treatment overall had no significant effect on the soluble protein content in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage (Fig. 3h).

In summary, MoCDs treatment significantly delayed postharvest senescence and quality deterioration of flowering Chinese cabbage. The appearance and nutritional quality of flowering Chinese cabbage treated with MoCDs were better than that of the control group during storage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MoCDs treatment delay the senescence and quality deterioration of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage by regulating the ROS metabolism
ce:section.ce:para: After harvest, fruits and vegetables remain alive and carry out various biosynthetic and metabolic activities, particularly, regulating ROS metabolism has become a key focus in postharvest treatments to control fruit senescence and quality deterioration (Meitha et al., 2020; Li et al., 2023). Therefore, we investigated whether the postharvest senescence and quality deterioration of flowering Chinese cabbage delayed by MoCDs treatment was associated with ROS homeostasis regulation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MoCDs treatment reduce ROS accumulation and alleviate membrane lipid peroxidation in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage
ce:section.ce:para:
Low levels of ROS function as signals to modulate plant functions, while excessive ROS accumulation causes oxidative damage (Li et al., 2023). Fig. 4 a shows the DAB staining results. On day 2, the control leaves displayed H2O2 accumulation, leading to brown patches. As the senescence progressed, the brown color on control leaves gradually deepened to almost complete browning by day 5. Fig. 4b illustrates similar NBT staining results in control. However, DAB and NBT staining colors and areas on the MoCDs-treated leaves were significantly lighter and smaller than those of the control group at the same storage period (Fig. 4a and b). The results of DAB and NBT staining indicated that MoCDs treatment effectively reduced ROS (H2O2 and
O 2
• ‐
) accumulation in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage during storage. We further quantitatively measured H2O2 content and
O 2
• ‐
production rate in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage (Fig. 4c and d). Consistent with the histochemical staining results, we found that the H2O2 content and
O 2
• ‐
production rate increased continuously in both treatments during storage. Still, MoCDs treatment significantly reduced the H2O2 content and
O 2
• ‐
production rate from day 2 to day 5 of storage, thereby maintaining a lower ROS level in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage.

Excessive ROS accumulation in plants can cause lipid peroxidation and damage the plant's biological membrane system (Tan et al., 2020). Malondialdehyde (MDA), being the ultimate product of lipid peroxidation, is an indicator for evaluating lipid peroxidation and biological membrane integrity (Du et al., 2024). In this study, the MDA content of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage in the control and MoCDs treatments showed a gradual upward trend during storage, similar to the ROS accumulation trend (Fig. 4e). MoCDs treatment significantly inhibited the increase of MDA content in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage in comparison to the control group, suggesting that MoCDs treatment alleviated lipid peroxidation of flowering Chinese cabbage. After five days of storage, MDA content in MoCDs treatment was 20.50% lower than in control.

Based on the above findings, we concluded that MoCDs treatment could reduce ROS accumulation in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage, alleviate membrane lipid peroxidation caused by excessive ROS accumulation, and protect the integrity of the biological membrane system, thus delaying postharvest senescence and maintaining the postharvest quality of the flowering Chinese cabbage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MoCDs treatment enhance the activities of antioxidant enzymes in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage
ce:section.ce:para:
Plants primarily fight against oxidative stress through an endogenous antioxidant defense system consisting of antioxidant enzymes and low-molecular-weight nonenzymatic antioxidants (Gill and Tuteja, 2010). Therefore, we further explored whether MoCDs decreased ROS accumulation in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage by regulating the antioxidant defense system. Superoxide dismutase (SOD) quickly converts
O 2
• ‐
into H2O2 and O2, and this generated H2O2 can be further converted into H2O by antioxidant enzymes such as catalase (CAT), peroxidase (POD), and glutathione peroxidase (GPX), or catalyzed in the ascorbate-glutathione cycle (AsA-GSH cycle) (Hasanuzzaman et al., 2021). In our study, the SOD, POD, and GPX activities of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage in both treatments showed gradually increasing trends during storage (Fig. 5 a–c). SOD activity of MoCDs treatment during 1–4 days of storage was significantly higher than that of control at the same time. Compared with the control group, the POD activity of MoCDs treatment increased by 37.03 %, 108.16 %, and 38.82 % after storing for 2, 3, and 4 days, respectively. Similarly, MoCDs treatment significantly increased the GPX activity in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage on days 2 and 3 of storage. In addition, MoCDs treatment could promote CAT activity more quickly than the control group, as evidenced by its earlier achievement of peak activity (Fig. 5d).

The AsA-GSH cycle is the major antioxidant defense pathway for detoxifying H2O2 and is beneficial to maintaining the redox homeostasis of plants (Hasanuzzaman et al., 2019). To study the effect of MoCDs treatment on the operational efficiency of the AsA-GSH cycle in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage, we measured the activities of APX, GR, MDHAR, and DHAR, as well as the contents of oxidation and reduction states of AsA and GSH. In our study, the APX activity of MoCDs treatment was significantly higher than that of the control on days 3–5 of storage (Fig. 5e). As shown in Fig. 5f and g, MoCDs treatment enhances GR and MDHAR activities and maintains higher GR and MDHAR activities compared with the control throughout storage. MoCDs treatment also significantly increased DHAR activity on days 3 and 4 of storage (Fig. 5h). In addition, AsA and GSH are powerful radical scavengers and directly react non-enzymatically with 1O2,
O 2
• ‐
, and •OH or reduce H2O2 to water via the AsA-GSH cycle (Hasanuzzaman et al., 2019). As shown in Fig. S5a, MoCDs treatment significantly increases AsA content compared to the control group throughout storage. In contrast, it decreased DHA levels, particularly on days 2 and 3 (Fig. S5b). Therefore, the MoCDs treatment maintained a high AsA/DHA ratio and a highly reduced state of the AsA pool (Fig. S5c). Similarly, MoCDs treatment increased GSH content except on day 2, and kept a high GSH/GSSG ratio and a highly reduced state of the GSH pool on days 1, 4, and 5 (Figs. S6a–S6c). Higher levels of AsA and GSH content, along with higher ratios of AsA/DHA and GSH/GSSG, reflect the redox state of plants, and a high reduction potential is conducive to plant resistance to oxidative stress (Tan et al., 2020).

In summary, as depicted in Fig. 5i, MoCDs treatment initially enhanced SOD activity, thereby accelerating the conversion of superoxide anion radical into hydrogen peroxide in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage. Subsequently, MoCDs treatment generally increased the activities of a series of key antioxidant enzymes, including POD, GPX, CAT, APX, DHAR, MDHAR, and GR, thus facilitating the efficient operation of the AsA-GSH cycle and detoxification of hydrogen peroxide in flowering Chinese cabbage. The cascade and coupling catalytic reactions of these MoCDs-enhanced antioxidant enzymes accelerated the regeneration of AsA and GSH, maintained high ratios of AsA/DHA and GSH/GSSG, thereby reducing ROS accumulation during storage, mitigating oxidative damage, and accordingly delaying postharvest senescence and quality deterioration of flowering Chinese cabbage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MoCDs treatment increase the contents of non-enzymatic antioxidants in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage
ce:section.ce:para: Besides antioxidant enzymes, low-molecular-weight non-enzymatic antioxidants (such as AsA, GSH, phenolic compounds, flavonoids, and tocopherols) are also beneficial for scavenging ROS and regulating ROS homeostasis (Khan et al., 2023). The physiological effects of MoCDs on AsA and GSH were described in detail in Section 3.4.2. Next, we investigated the impact of MoCDs treatment on other non-enzymatic antioxidants. Phenolic compounds are a variety of secondary metabolites produced by phenylpropanoid metabolism and trap lipid alkoxy radicals to prevent lipid peroxidation (Yu et al., 2022). In our study, total phenolic content in MoCDs treatment was increased by 17.66 %, 7.49 %, and 8.64 % after storing for 3, 4, and 5 days compared with the control, respectively, suggesting that MoCDs could significantly promote the accumulation of phenolic compounds at the later storage (Fig. 6 a). Flavonoids, a type of phenolic compound, can directly scavenge ROS or act as substrates for various peroxidases (Yu et al., 2022). Similarly, MoCDs treatment also significantly increased the total flavonoid content of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage during storage (Fig. 6b). Furthermore, tocopherols are lipophilic antioxidants and directly scavenge ROS and lipid radicals to form quinones, epoxides, and tocopherol radicals, thereby alleviating lipid peroxidation and protecting cell biological membranes (Khan et al., 2023). In our study, we found that MoCDs treatment increased the content of tocopherols in the early storage, thereby reducing the accumulation of ROS and alleviating lipid peroxidation (Fig. 6c).

In short, during storage, MoCDs treatment significantly increased the content of non-enzymatic antioxidants in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage to varying degrees, including total phenolics, total flavonoids, and tocopherols, thereby effectively boosting the antioxidant ability of flowering Chinese cabbage. Among them, the increase in the content of lipophilic antioxidants (particularly phenolic compounds, flavonoids, and tocopherols) was beneficial for alleviating lipid peroxidation by capturing lipid radicals, thereby protecting the integrity of the cell membrane structure and function.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MoCDs treatment significantly enhance the antioxidant ability of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage
ce:section.ce:para: In the above discussion, we demonstrated that MoCDs treatment could increase the antioxidant enzyme activities and non-enzymatic antioxidant contents, thereby improving the ROS scavenging ability of endogenous antioxidant defense systems. The tests of the antioxidant ability of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage further proved these results. As illustrated in Fig. 6d, the •OH scavenging rate in the control and MoCDs treatments gradually increases overall during storage. The •OH scavenging rate of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage treated with MoCDs was consistently higher than that of the control group except on day 2. Similarly, the DPPH• scavenging rate in the control and MoCDs treatments showed an increasing trend, while MoCDs treatment enhanced the DPPH• scavenging rate compared with the control during five days of storage (Fig. 6e). In addition, the FRAP (Ferric Reducing Antioxidant Power) assay can evaluate the electron-donating capacity of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage by monitoring the absorbance changes of the Fe2+ complexes generated during the reaction (Li et al., 2023). As shown in Fig. 6f, the FRAP value of MoCDs treatment significantly increases on days 3–5 of storage compared with the control group. This finding suggested that postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage treated with MoCDs had higher reducing power at the later storage.

Our study successfully synthesized M. oleifera leaf-derived carbon dots (MoCDs) with excellent antioxidant activity and good biocompatibility. MoCDs showed more efficient antioxidant activity than M. oleifera extract due to their rich surface functional groups and large conjugated sp2-hybridized carbon framework, making them a promising tool for postharvest preservation of flowering Chinese cabbage (Fig. 7 a). The results showed that MoCDs treatment significantly delayed postharvest senescence and quality deterioration of flowering Chinese cabbage during storage by regulating ROS metabolism (Fig. 7b). Specifically, as an effective antioxidant, MoCDs with small size, excellent aqueous dispersibility, and negative charge easily entered leaf cells to scavenge excessive ROS directly. Concurrently, as an elicitor, MoCDs treatment increased the activities of antioxidant enzymes and the contents of non-enzymatic antioxidants, thus improving the ROS scavenging ability of endogenous antioxidant defense systems of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage. These dual protective ways worked together to enhance the antioxidant ability of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage treated with MoCDs, subsequently reducing ROS accumulation and alleviating membrane lipid peroxidation induced by excessive ROS, thereby protecting the integrity of the biological membrane system, delaying leaf senescence and deterioration of appearance and nutritional quality of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage during storage. Our study results demonstrate significant practical application value in enhancing the commercial quality of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage, providing a scientific basis for improving the postharvest preservation of horticultural crops using biomass-derived carbon dots, expecting to promote the development and application of novel biomass-derived carbon dots in the horticultural crop preservation field.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
